title: Dopamine Agonists: Risks, Adverse Effects, Surveillance, and Intervention

items.title: Why cabergoline is preferred over bromocriptine for prolactinomas
items.text: Cabergoline normalizes prolactin and shrinks tumors in >90% of patients with better tolerability than bromocriptine (80–90% normalization, ~50% >50% shrinkage).
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Cabergoline, in use since the mid-1990s, can achieve these goals in more than 90% of patients."

items.title: How dopamine agonists cause impulse control disorders
items.text: Interaction with mesolimbic D3 receptors drives gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive eating/shopping, and punding—seen in 15–20% of treated patients, somewhat dose dependent.
items.reference: Case 1: "Several studies have shown that both cabergoline and bromocriptine can cause compulsive behavior in 15% to 20% of treated patients."

items.title: CSF rhinorrhea after macroadenoma shrinkage on dopamine agonists
items.text: Large skull-base invasive macroadenomas can develop CSF fistula as tumor shrinks—diagnose with β2-transferrin in nasal fluid and repair to prevent meningitis.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "shrinkage of a large skull-based macroadenoma that invades the sellar floor can result in the leakage of cerebrospinal fluid around the now smaller tumor."

items.title: Cardiac valve risk with high-dose cabergoline
items.text: 15–20% of prolactinoma patients need >standard dosing; Parkinson doses (3–5 mg daily) associate with valve disease via 5-HT2B receptors—echocardiography when crossing >2 mg/week is recommended.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "About 15% to 20% of patients require larger than standard dosages of cabergoline to normalize prolactin levels, and large dosages may cause cardiac valve abnormalities."

items.title: How to manage dopamine agonists in pregnancy
items.text: Both drugs appear safe for the fetus, but stop cabergoline when pregnancy is confirmed—microadenoma enlargement risk ~2% (symptoms only) vs macroadenoma ~18% (visual fields each trimester).
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "visual field testing each trimester in addition to symptomatic monitoring is necessary for those with macroadenomas, as their risk for tumor enlargement is 18%."

items.title: How to manage cabergoline-resistant prolactinoma
items.text: Increase cabergoline stepwise documenting prolactin response—~50% of bromocriptine failures respond to cabergoline; surgery or radiosurgery are alternatives.
items.reference: Case 2: "most patients who have some response to cabergoline will have a further response if the dosage is increased."

items.title: Silent gonadotroph adenomas among "nonfunctioning" tumors
items.text: 40–75% of clinically nonfunctioning adenomas are gonadotroph adenomas secreting subunits or intact gonadotropins without clinical syndrome.
items.reference: Case 5: "Between 40% and 75% of clinically nonfunctioning adenomas are actually gonadotroph adenomas"

items.title: Why a spouse may ask to reduce cabergoline dose
items.text: Hypersexuality is more common in men (6.5% isolated in one prolactinoma cohort)—impulse control disorders may be hidden; ask patient and partner at follow-up.
items.reference: Case 1: "Hypersexuality was more common in men, and compulsive eating was more common in women."

items.title: Echocardiography threshold for hyperprolactinemic cabergoline patients
items.text: No increased valve abnormalities vs controls when doses stay ≤2 mg/week in most studies—but perform echo when exceeding 2 mg/week then annually.
items.reference: Case 2: "hyperprolactinemic patients receiving greater than 2 mg weekly of cabergoline have echocardiography performed when that dosage threshold is crossed with then annually thereafter."

items.title: Why cabergoline is stopped when pregnancy is confirmed
items.text: Despite fetal safety data, standard practice is to discontinue dopamine agonists in pregnancy and monitor for symptomatic tumor growth.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: C) Stop cabergoline"

items.title: How to confirm CSF rhinorrhea
items.text: β2-transferrin measurement in nasal fluid confirms CSF fistula—leak must be surgically repaired.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea can be diagnosed by measuring β2-transferrin in the nasal fluid, and the leak must be repaired to prevent meningitis."

items.title: Pitfall: "nonfunctioning" macroadenoma with mild hyperprolactinemia
items.text: Immunostaining may reveal silent lactotroph adenoma—this patient's tumor stained for prolactin and stabilized on long-term cabergoline despite minimal prolactin elevation preoperatively.
items.reference: Case 5: "the patient's tumor stained positively not for gonadotropins but for prolactin, so he had a "silent" lactotroph adenoma."

items.question: A man's wife asks about reducing cabergoline after prolactin normalization and tumor shrinkage. She is most concerned about:
items.explanation: Impulse control disorders including hypersexuality are recognized cabergoline adverse effects.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Hypersexuality"

items.question: A woman on cabergoline has prolactin 68 ng/mL (partial shrinkage, no menses). Best next step?
items.explanation: Stepwise cabergoline escalation is appropriate when partial response exists.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: C) Increase the cabergoline dosage"

items.question: A woman 20 weeks pregnant on long-term cabergoline for macroprolactinoma. Best management?
options: Switch to bromocriptine and continue throughout pregnancy
Stop now but restart if symptomatic
items.explanation: Stop dopamine agonist when pregnancy is confirmed; monitor macroadenoma with visual fields.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: C) Stop cabergoline"

items.question: After incomplete resection of a "nonfunctioning" macroadenoma with minimal prolactin elevation, immunostaining is most likely positive for:
items.explanation: Most clinically NFPA are gonadotroph adenomas staining for α subunit; this case was silent lactotroph.
items.reference: Case 5: "Answer: A) Positive stain for a subunit"

items.question: Cabergoline-associated valve disease is thought to involve:
options: D2 receptor blockade in myocardium only
5-HT2B receptor activation causing mitogenic leaflet changes
items.explanation: Serotonin 5-HT2B pathway on valves resembles carcinoid-like plaque.
items.reference: Case 2: "cabergoline has action at the serotonin (5-HT) 2B receptors, which are present in valves"

items.question: Risk of symptomatic prolactinoma enlargement during pregnancy is approximately:
items.explanation: Microadenomas need symptomatic monitoring; macroadenomas need trimester visual fields.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "their risk for tumor enlargement is only about 2%, visual field testing each trimester in addition to symptomatic monitoring is necessary for those with macroadenomas, as their risk for tumor enlargement is 18%."

items.question: Approximately what fraction of prolactinoma patients require more than standard cabergoline dosing?
items.explanation: 15–20% are resistant to conventional ≤2 mg/week dosing.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "About 15% to 20% of patients require larger than standard dosages of cabergoline to normalize prolactin levels"

items.question: In bromocriptine nonresponders, approximately what fraction respond to cabergoline?
items.explanation: About half of bromocriptine failures respond to cabergoline.
items.reference: Case 2: "about 50% of patients who do not respond to bromocriptine do respond to cabergoline."

items.question: CSF rhinorrhea after DA-induced tumor shrinkage is confirmed by:
items.explanation: β2-transferrin is diagnostic for CSF fistula.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea can be diagnosed by measuring β2-transferrin in the nasal fluid"

items.question: In a Turkish prolactinoma cohort, overall impulse control disorder prevalence was:
items.explanation: 17% had ICDs including hypersexuality, gambling, eating, shopping.
items.reference: Case 1: "17% were found to have impulse control disorders"

items.question: If valve abnormalities develop on high-dose cabergoline in Parkinson disease:
options: About one-third regress if drug stopped
items.explanation: Limited data: one-third regress, half stable off drug; bromocriptine lacks this association.
items.reference: Case 2: "if valve abnormalities develop, one-third have regression and half have lack of further progression of valve disease if the drug is stopped."

items.question: Regarding dopamine agonists in pregnancy:
options: Both increase major fetal malformation rates significantly
Neither has been shown to increase fetal malformation risk
Only bromocriptine is safe; cabergoline is contraindicated
Both must be continued through delivery
items.explanation: Main conclusions state neither increases malformation risk.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Neither drug has been shown to increase the risk of fetal malformations."

items.question: Common dopamine agonist nausea is best managed by:
options: Low starting dose with gradual escalation over weeks to months
Permanent drug discontinuation at first nausea
items.explanation: Gradual titration while monitoring prolactin is standard.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "The common adverse effect of nausea can be lessened by starting with low dosages and gradually increasing over weeks to months, monitoring the prolactin response."

items.statement: Cabergoline achieves prolactin normalization in more than 90% of prolactinoma patients.
items.explanation: Stated in significance section.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Cabergoline, in use since the mid-1990s, can achieve these goals in more than 90% of patients."

items.statement: Most echocardiographic studies show increased valve disease in hyperprolactinemic patients on ≤2 mg/week cabergoline versus controls.
items.explanation: Almost all studies show no increase at ≤2 mg/week.
items.reference: Case 2: "Almost all echocardiographic studies have not shown an increase in valve abnormalities in hyperprolactinemic patients taking cabergoline compared with control patients when dosages are kept to a maximum of 2 mg weekly."

items.statement: Hypersexuality is more commonly reported in women than men on dopamine agonists.
items.explanation: Hypersexuality more common in men in cited study.
items.reference: Case 1: "Hypersexuality was more common in men"

items.statement: Untreated CSF rhinorrhea after prolactinoma shrinkage risks meningitis.
items.explanation: Leak must be repaired to prevent meningitis.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "the leak must be repaired to prevent meningitis."

items.statement: Bromocriptine and cabergoline are considered safe for the developing fetus.
items.explanation: Significance section affirms fetal safety.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Both bromocriptine and cabergoline are safe for the developing fetus when given to women who wish to become pregnant."

items.statement: Pregnant women with macroprolactinomas require visual field testing each trimester.
items.explanation: 18% enlargement risk mandates formal perimetry.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "visual field testing each trimester in addition to symptomatic monitoring is necessary for those with macroadenomas"

items.statement: A completely hormone-negative pituitary adenoma immunostain is the most common result in clinically nonfunctioning tumors.
items.explanation: Negative stain for all hormones is uncommon; gonadotroph adenomas predominate.
items.reference: Case 5: "A negative stain for all pituitary hormones (Answer D) is uncommon."

items.statement: Impulse control disorders may be hidden from family and require direct questioning.
items.explanation: Clinicians should warn patients and ask spouses at visits.
items.reference: Case 1: "It is important to also ask the spouse, as some impulse control disorders, such as gambling and compulsive shopping, may be hidden from the family."

items.assertion: Assertion: High-dose cabergoline may cause cardiac valve abnormalities.
items.reason: Reason: Cabergoline activates 5-HT2B receptors on valves triggering mitogenic changes.
items.explanation: Both true—Parkinson data inform prolactinoma high-dose risk.
items.reference: Case 2: "cabergoline has action at the serotonin (5-HT) 2B receptors, which are present in valves"

items.assertion: Assertion: Cabergoline should be stopped when pregnancy is confirmed.
items.reason: Reason: Cabergoline is proven teratogenic and must be switched to bromocriptine.
items.explanation: Assertion true per case 3; fetal malformation risk not shown—reason false.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Neither drug has been shown to increase the risk of fetal malformations."

items.assertion: Assertion: Increasing cabergoline may normalize prolactin in partial responders.
items.reason: Reason: Patients with no response to cabergoline never benefit from higher doses.
items.explanation: Assertion true for partial responders; reason false.
items.reference: Case 2: "most patients who have some response to cabergoline will have a further response if the dosage is increased."

items.assertion: Assertion: Dopamine agonist-induced tumor shrinkage can cause CSF rhinorrhea.
items.reason: Reason: Shrinkage of invasive macroadenomas can leave a CSF leak around the smaller tumor.
items.explanation: Both true per main conclusions.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "shrinkage of a large skull-based macroadenoma that invades the sellar floor can result in the leakage of cerebrospinal fluid"

items.assertion: Assertion: Dopamine agonists can cause pathologic gambling and hypersexuality.
items.reason: Reason: These behaviors result from D3 receptor interaction in the mesolimbic system.
items.explanation: Both true—mechanism and prevalence cited.
items.reference: Case 1: "The mechanism of action behind impulse control disorders seems to be an interaction between the dopamine agonists and the D_{3} receptors in the mesolimbic system"

items.assertion: Assertion: Most clinically nonfunctioning adenomas are gonadotroph adenomas.
items.reason: Reason: They always secrete clinically evident FSH excess causing OHSS.
items.explanation: Assertion true; functioning OHSS is very rare—reason false.
items.reference: Case 5: "Between 40% and 75% of clinically nonfunctioning adenomas are actually gonadotroph adenomas"

items.assertion: Assertion: Echocardiography is recommended when cabergoline exceeds 2 mg weekly.
items.reason: Reason: All patients on any cabergoline dose have 34% tricuspid regurgitation.
items.explanation: Assertion true per guidelines; 34% is Parkinson high-dose data—not all prolactinoma doses.
items.reference: Case 2: "hyperprolactinemic patients receiving greater than 2 mg weekly of cabergoline have echocardiography performed when that dosage threshold is crossed"

items.assertion: Assertion: Bromocriptine is associated with cardiac valve abnormalities like high-dose cabergoline.
items.reason: Reason: Valve abnormalities are not seen with bromocriptine.
items.explanation: Assertion false; reason true.
items.reference: Case 2: "Valve abnormalities are not seen with bromocriptine."
